data.title: Gold: Wars And Jungle Economics
data.description:
It is just about a year ago since my previous article on gold on April 10th, 2025, when gold was trading around the $3,000 level and we only had one big war in the Ukraine grinding away at geopolitical stability. On 28 February 2026, the USA and Israel launched a war against Iran to open a 2nd large war front globally. In both cases the wars are referred to as “military operations”.

Global economic benefits, after WWII, were greatly advanced through the discouragement of wars and the avoidance of international aggression while encouraging international trade. Geopolitical stability was seen as essential to economic recovery and progress.

UN Resolutions adopted on the reports of the Sixth Committee, Page 143.

Is Geopolitical Stability or Instability a Primary Driver of the Price of Gold?

The most important economic variable for all forms of economic progress and advancement, in my opinion, is economic cooperation. Economic cooperation under the conditions of freedom of choice and absent coercion, leverage, force or any interference with free will. Unbridled freedom is unrealistic, but the closer economic cooperation is to freedom of choice the more economically beneficial it becomes.

All forms of trade are economic cooperation. All forms of production are economic cooperation. The greater international cooperation, the greater economic specialization. The greater economic specialization, the greater economic interdependence. The greater economic interdependence the less likely it is for countries to go to war.

Cooperation yields incredible economic benefits including peace, specialization, economies of scale, efficient capital allocation, international standardization and a legion more. Remove cooperation from any form of economic activity, and it will be greatly diminished if not destroyed completely.

International Law and the activities of countries under the umbrella of the United Nations created an international cooperation framework vital to the economic health of the nations. It is very far from perfect and often hopelessly inadequate but still better than international chaos where countries act under the Laws of the Jungle. The strong shall take all and rule over everybody. The survival of the fittest. If you can’t hold it, you can’t have it. If you can’t defend it, you lose it. The weak shall be the slave to the strong. All for me, none for you. To the victor goes the spoils. More along those lines of thought.

The Law of the Jungle is the antithesis of economic cooperation and a rise of “jungle behavior” in global economics destroys economic cooperation. Gold is the ultimate asset to hold under jungle economics. The classic geopolitical character of gold can be measured in international economic cooperation. The greater the cooperation the less interest (or need) there is in (for) gold but the less international cooperation the greater the demand (and need) for gold.

International cooperation goes in steep decline when a preference for the Law of the Jungle emerges. Jungle economics will result in a general economic decline but will also stimulate increased macro demand for gold.

Gold, Geopolitics and the Law of the Jungle.

International order and the United Nations

The United Nations functions or fails on cooperation. It has very limited enforcement powers and the economically and military dominant countries have increasingly veered away from cooperation towards Laws of the Jungle. Under cooperation they would seek to improve international structures when those structures are functionally weak or broken, not actively work towards destroying what little functionally there is. The ultimate betrayal of cooperation under the umbrella of the structures and institutions of the United Nations is a failure to pay the United Nations dues. A bankrupt United Nations is the final stage of “every nation for itself under the Laws of the Jungle”. As the UN general secretary warned on 30 January, 20206, this risks 'imminent financial collapse':

The United Nations is at risk of "imminent financial collapse" due to member states not paying their fees, the body's head has warned.

António Guterres said the UN faced a financial crisis which was "deepening, threatening programme delivery", and that money could run out by July.

He wrote in a letter to all 193 member states that they had to honour their mandatory payments or overhaul the organisation's financial rules to avoid collapse.

It comes after the UN's largest contributor, the US, refused to contribute to its regular and peacekeeping budgets, and withdrew from several agencies it called a "waste of taxpayer dollars". Several other members are in arrears or are simply refusing to pay.

International Gold Barter (International trade settlement in gold)

Believing that international gold barter is something which had died at Bretton Woods, when a gold backed US dollar became the international trade currency, is a mistake. Further believing that international gold barter was permanently banished in 1971, when Nixon resiled on international gold redemption of the US dollar, would be another mistake.

“Money” is simply a temporary IOU, an intermediary for barter. The ultimate objective is a swap of goods for goods. Gold still does this best when secrecy is required.

Iran, ironically, is a good example of the importance of international gold barter. Sanctions, and specifically financial sanctions, exclude countries from the international financial system and prevent trade payments through the international settlement systems. Bartering gold for goods leaves no paper trail and physical settlement takes place as goods for goods. Gold moves to national gold reserves and nation states settle distribution via its national financial systems.

Aljazeera reports on increased gold transactions by Iran. They also report that the increased gold activity is tied to international trade settlement including weapons trade. Iran as a result bets on gold.

Some reports have indicated Iran got part of the gold from Russia in exchange for suicide drones. Iranian officials have not commented on the claim, maintaining that they only exported some drones to Russia months before the start of the Ukraine war.

Details are scant as physical settlement with gold for other goods happens in secret. That secrecy is visible also in Iran’s official gold reserve reporting which stopped at 1982 (World Bank Data: Total Reserves, Iran, Islamic Republic) and gold production which is an estimate at around 7,000kg (225,055.2 oz) per annum worth over $1bn per annum. It is easy to envisage Iran bartering oil for goods and gold with China and then bartering gold for goods with Russia. All off the record, all physical settlement.

Have gold, can barter, can trade, can pay…

Jungle Economics

A world divided by wars, renewed colonization, imperialism and extreme self-interest objectives will increasingly turn to gold to settle international trade off-the-books. It is not only about secrecy. Trust becomes an absolute requirement. Physical gold settlement for international trade is the ultimate form of trusted payment. There is no fiat money payment, no reliance on the value of another’s currency or banking system. The exchange is goods for goods and settled in full, intrinsic value for intrinsic value.

Gold and the International Monetary System.

I have discussed the international monetary system in detail in my article “Global Financial Markets: Slow Motion Crash Underway”, which please refer to in need.

It follows from the discussion above that the international dollar-based fiat monetary system will increasingly become decrepit as nations elect to use alternative fiat currencies of close trading partners, such as the Yuan, or barter trade arrangements.

A fragmented international monetary system across a greater number of trade currencies will materially affect the US economic system which is utterly dependent upon the US dollar as dominant international trade currency. The long-time (since 1971) USA trade deficits are funded through the US dollar monetary system. The $39 trillion (more than 100% of GDP) USA national debt is funded through the US dollar monetary system. The US stock exchange and financial markets are. also dependent upon the circular flow of US dollar through the US dollar monetary system. (All explained in detail in the article quoted above.)

Fragmentation of the international monetary system together with the erosion of international economic cooperation will accelerate adoption of alternative international trade settlements to the benefit of gold.

More important is a decline in the standing of the US dollar as fiat. A realignment of trade settlement in dollar alternatives undermines trust in fiat currencies globally which, in turn, will increase demand for national gold reserves and support central bank purchases of gold.

MacroMicro

The chart reflects net purchases of gold by Central Banks which are now trending comfortably above 200 tonnes per quarter. The trend in Central Bank gold purchases changed with the re-emergence of inflation globally as a result of yet another round of crisis money creation during and after COVID. Less international cooperation, more war, more defense/war spending, more government budget deficits and more need for government debt will encourage ever more Central Bank gold purchases.

The settlement of international trade is a traditional role of gold stretching back more than 4,000 years. Settlement in pure fiat is a recent development and fiat systems, historically, have always failed. Unrestrained money creation prevalent in fiat systems eventually always destroyed trust in a money not backed by assets of intrinsic value.

I do not view an asset such as bitcoin as a viable alternative to gold as discussed in my article on bitcoin, Cryptocurrencies: Monetary Alternative Or Mass Delusion?

Inflation

Wars are expensive. Waging war with dated equipment is doubly so. The Ukraine war already warned that the new battlefield is no longer a WWII arena. Drones and AI have taken over. Tanks, battle ships, bombers, fighter aircraft and all large equipment are heading to the scrapyard. A new arms race to win in the new battle arena is already underway globally. Fall behind and you become easy prey in a Laws of the Jungle world.

That means more budget deficits globally, more government debt formation globally, larger defense budgets and larger government interest burdens. Governments no longer even attempt to fund spending excesses through taxation, its just too politically unpalatable. Governments simply turn to debt and sell the debt to the central banks. The central banks just create new fiat for all that new debt and dilute the purchasing power of everybody in the process. That is inflationary across everything.

The wars also interfere directly in supply chains on energy and on all derivative products from specifically oil. Prices increase structurally. Here fertilizer is a red flag for food inflation. Destruction of infrastructure on oil and oil derivatives will not be repaired overnight once the wars end. Supply chains will probably take years to return to pre-war supply.

A Monetary Inflation Shock and a Supply Chain Price Shock Combined

It is early days on inflation data. February US inflation (CPI) is stable at 2.4%. US producer inflation (PPI) for February came in hot even before the Iran war impact.

US producer prices rose 0.7% month-over-month in February 2026, above 0.5% in January and much higher than forecasts of 0.3%. It is the biggest increase in producer prices in seven months, with goods prices soaring 1.1% the most since August 2023, led by a 48.9% jump in prices for fresh and dry vegetables.

EU inflation (CPI) is already increasing, and the EU is particularly vulnerable to a new round of inflation from the Iran war.

Euro area annual inflation is expected to be 2.5% in March 2026, up from 1.9% in February according to a flash estimate from Eurostat, the statistical office of the European Union.

Looking at the main components of euro area inflation, energy is expected to have the highest annual rate in March (4.9%, compared with -3.1% in February), followed by services (3.2%, compared with 3.4% in February), food, alcohol & tobacco (2.4%, compared with 2.5% in February) and non-energy industrial goods (0.5%, compared with 0.7% in February).

Euro area annual inflation up to 2.5%. The latest EU producer data is for January 2026 and not useful at this stage. New inflation data is imminent and is expected to arrive and remain elevated for some time.

Gold should do very well once the post war inflation data becomes available. The first hit will come from the supply chain, but the trend will change due to the increased government deficit spending on war, arms renewal and rising interest cost from increased borrowing and increased interest rates.

Oil/Gold Inversion

A short-term peculiarity of the Iran war is the divergence between the oil price and the gold price. Gold and oil prices, before the Iran war, were in lockstep but have diverged with oil moving up (war premium) and gold declining. I could speculate as to the reasons, but it would not serve any actionable purpose. The effect currently is that the gold price declines when the oil price increases (usually as expectations for a short easy war converts to a longer, more complex engagement). I expect that this divergence will revert over time back to lockstep with oil lower and gold higher. I see this as a short-term purchasing opportunity.

The chart shows a comparison between the gold futures (GC) and Brent oil futures (LCO) and the divergence is very obvious.

Risks to the Bullish Thesis on Gold

The most obvious risk is a return to global cooperation and stability. I do not see that risk as significant in the near term as current geopolitical trends are moving in the opposite direction. A good example is the friction between the USA and the rest of the NATO countries.

Any resolution of the wars in the Ukraine and in Iran has the potential to negatively impact the gold price provided that it combines with a return to global cooperation and stability. The short term impact of a reduction in hostilities with Iran is expected to be gold price positive given the oil/gold price inversion discussed above.

The impact of deficits, debt formation, inflation, supply chain stress and an arms race on the gold price is a near certainty and there are no credible alternative strategies to these trends which are already entrenched.

Risk to the bullish thesis is considered low at this juncture.

Conclusion

Gold is supported by positive long-term macro-economic variables which underpins its demand and price. These mega trends are in core geopolitical gold price drivers, the rise of Jungle Economics, declining international cooperation, rising international barter trade and gold settlement and declining international trust.

Wars exposed the large-scale redundancy of existing military equipment which initiated an arms race of military renewal. Governments are already overindebted and underfunded but will nevertheless forge ahead with new debt and central bank funding to avoid falling behind on defense in a world trending towards Laws of the Jungle.

Deficit spending, trade deficits and interest costs will trigger another round of fiat money creation to advance inflation generally. War consequences on the supply chain, from destruction and disruption, will not disappear overnight once (when) the war is over but is expected to take years to reconstruct.

The Iran war created a short-term gold price decline which is not expected to last and offers an attractive new entry point for gold.

The long-term big picture for gold is compelling and foundational which earns a “strong buy” rating on gold from me.

data.title: Jefferies: Wall Street Booms, But Private Credit Woes Weigh
data.description:
Capital markets stocks have been hit hard by private credit woes. Much ink has been spilled regarding the travails of Blue Owl (OWL) and, to a lesser extent, KKR (KKR), Ares (ARES), and Apollo (APO). Shares of Jefferies Financial Group Inc. (JEF), for instance, are down sharply from their late-2024 highs. The drawdown briefly reached 50% at the lows last month.

Now, after a rebound and a solid earnings report, JEF has been on the near-term mend. I had a Buy rating on the $9 billion market cap Financials sector stock back in October, near the Q4 lows. Shares are down 13% since then, but I reiterate a Buy rating. I see value in the beaten-up Investment Banking and Brokerage industry equity.

With hopes of a geopolitical resolution and the possibility of improved IPO activity amid ongoing decent investment banking trends (including very high M&A volume), I assert the market is mispricing JEF. I’ll provide an updated valuation and a fresh look at the technicals.

JEF Recovering From Its March Low

In March, Jefferies reported a solid set of quarterly results, with a 27% YoY increase in net revenue to $2.0 billion and a rise in diluted EPS to $0.70. Growth was primarily fueled by record-breaking performance in its Investment Banking segment, where net sales reached $1.02 billion, driven by surging advisory and equity underwriting activity. Specifically, its equity underwriting grew fees collected by a whopping 138%, while advisory fees climbed 38% vs. Q1 2025.

Shares rallied 1.6% in the session that followed, and the options market prices in a somewhat high 5.6% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the June 24 reporting date. Implied volatility is elevated at 42%, and short interest on the stock is somewhat low at 2.4%.

While the company’s Equities Trading segment performed well with 37% growth from the same period a year earlier, the Fixed Income segment faced headwinds, including a $42.8 million write-down related to the collapse of Market Financial Solutions (MFS), the property lender that collapsed in February.

During the quarter, however, the firm continued its capital return strategy by repurchasing 3 million shares for $174 million and maintaining a $0.40 quarterly dividend, while also progressing on the wind-down of legacy merchant banking assets through the sale of Tessellis.

Overall, the bottom line was indeed pressured by Tessellis, which included a $36 million goodwill write-down and losses tied to First Brands and MFS. These factors, among others, drove a somewhat light EPS figure of $0.70, below analysts’ estimates. Overall, Jefferies improved its return on adjusted tangible equity to 10.9%, though, and signaled confidence in its core business.

Jefferies: Business Outlook

Back at the October 2025 Investor Meeting, CEO Richard Handler and other executives highlighted strength across most segments. The team noted the company’s transformation into a leading pure-play global investment banking and capital markets firm. According to the presentation, Jefferies reached its strongest market position ever, ranking #6 globally in investment banking revenue as of Q3 2025, up from #17 in 2010.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be very strong in FY 2026 and through the out year. The firm could tag $5 in operating per-share earnings by late next year on a trailing 12-month basis. But the sell side has soured on JEF, given four EPS downgrades in the past 90 days, compared to a lone upgrade.

Jefferies: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I don’t have wholesale changes to my October 2025 assessment. If we assume $4 of normalized non-GAAP EPS and apply a 15x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $60. That makes the stock about 25% undervalued today. JEF also trades cheaply on a price-to-book basis.

JEF: Compelling On A P/E And P/B Basis

Key risks for Jefferies include weaker capital markets activity—a global growth slowdown could weigh on IPOs, M&A, and equity & debt underwriting. For now, though, most signals point to continued strength, given the momentum in overall dealmaking.

Higher interest rates are another possible threat—we have seen global intermediate sovereign yields rise, which effectively tightens financial conditions. Finally, private credit must be monitored for further “cockroaches,” while AI investment may be excessive, also pressuring capital markets going forward.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q2 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, June 24, for AMC. Shares trade ex a $0.40 quarterly dividend on Monday, May 18.

Corporate Event Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares even further from my fair value estimate, JEF’s technical situation is not all that great. Notice in the chart below that the $39 level I flagged last time was breached, in what now appears to be a potential bullish false breakdown. The stock fell into a congestion zone, as evidenced by a high amount of volume by price from back in 2021-2023. But with a falling long-term 200-day moving average, the bears appear to be in control of the primary trend.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. Momentum has repeatedly tested technical oversold conditions—that makes me wary of the current bounce. Adding to concerns is that JEF remains below the apex point of the 2025-2026 wide symmetrical triangle pattern. Overall, support is near $36, while resistance is near $50.

JEF: Bullish False Breakdown Potential, $50 Resistance, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on Jefferies. The stock chart is not pretty, and its relative weakness must not go unnoticed. The valuation is very attractive, though, particularly amid an outright bull market in Wall Street capital markets activity. Of course, the private credit saga must be monitored. Be on the lookout for volatility catalysts next week when the big banks report January-March results.

data.title: The Williams Companies: I See Quality, Just Not Enough Upside
data.description:
Thesis

I think that the easiest way to think about The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB) is they’re basically a giant toll-road company for natural gas. They usually aren’t the ones drilling the gas out of the ground, and they usually aren’t the utility selling it to your house either. However, they possess the pipes, storage facilities, processing equipment, and other infrastructure required to transport natural gas to the areas it is needed including generating stations, LNG export terminals, manufacturing centers, and local utility companies.

Their crown jewel is Transco, which is a huge pipeline system running from Texas up to New York City. In addition, they also have gathering and processing facilities located in leading gas-production areas, allowing them to gather raw gas from wellheads, process it for shipment, and deliver it to consumers.

A new factor now is power.

Williams has begun developing what it calls a “power innovation business.” In simple terms, it’s using its natural gas supply and turbine know-how to provide electricity solutions for very large energy users, such as data centers.

Recently, WMB’s been on my radar as it broke a key resistance level, up around 20% YTD. This is my first time covering the company, and after going over the most relevant fundamentals and data, I’m leaning neutral given the strong business quality but limited upside at current valuations.

The Williams Companies: Power Platform Scaling & Monetization

  • Power innovation total investment: $7.3B
  • Power innovation EBITDA (run-rate by 2029): ~$1.4B
  • Power project capacity (executed PPAs): ~1.3 GW
  • New project (Socrates the Younger): 340 MW, $1.3B
  • Additional project expansion (Aquila & Apollo): +$0.9B
  • Power innovation contract terms: 10–12.5 years
  • Build multiples (power projects): ~5x EBITD.

In addition to maintaining the strength of the legacy pipe business during the quarter, another key takeaway from Williams is that their power innovations business has begun to become much more of a legitimate profit driver, and no longer viewed as having been just a side project through this quarter.

  • Take-or-pay EBITDA share by 2030: >60%
  • EBITDA growth target (2025–2030): >10% CAGR
  • Base growth from current projects: ~8% CAGR.

Management is stating that not only is the quality of revenue increasing due to the regulated or take or pay structure of transmission, it is also beginning to see its next level of growth signed under long term contracts for power, creating a utility type cash flow instead of commodity exposed midstream-type upside. This is important because Williams has historically been rewarded by the market for its durability, but expanding this platform will produce further enhancement to that durability instead of dilution.

  • Adjusted EBITDA (2025): $7.75B
  • EBITDA growth (2025): ~9% YoY
  • Transmission & Gulf EBITDA growth (2025): +$400M (~12%)
  • Northeast G&P growth (2025): +$62M (~3%)
  • West segment growth (2025): +$128M (~10%)
  • Other segment growth (2025): +$100M
  • Q4 EBITDA (2025): >$2B.

Based on the transcript, it appears that transmission continued to be the primary earnings driver for the quarter and was supported by multiple project completions, increased system utilization, strong storage performance, and the benefit of prior commercial work flowing into results. There were also mixed results in gathering and processing operations by basin, while marketing normalized from a tougher comparison. One major qualitative theme for the quarter is that growth was primarily driven by volume and project activity, rather than a more supportive commodity environment.

  • Operating margin (2025): 75%
  • G&P excess cash flow (2025): ~$2.5B
  • Growth CapEx (2026): $6.4B
  • Maintenance CapEx (2026): $0.9B.

I also think there’s a solid argument that margins and cash flow are being driven more by product composition than by scale. Management pointed to the increased contribution from its transmission and Gulf assets, steady cash flow from gathering and processing, and solid operational discipline to fund heavy capital investments without showing an excessive cost structure. However, I wouldn't take the margin expansion story at face value. Some of the apparent quality improvement is due to leaning more heavily into businesses with better contract terms and lower earnings volatility.

  • Leverage (2025): ~3.7x EBITDA
  • Leverage target: 3.5x–4.0x.

This is good news. But it moves execution risk upstream into the delivery of projects. If construction gets delayed, if the connection work takes longer than expected, or if there aren’t enough workers or equipment available, profits can drop quickly. This happens because all the money for the project has already been spent upfront, while the income from the project has not fully come in yet.

  • Pipeline expansions in execution: ~7.1 Bcf/day
  • Pipeline backlog: >14 Bcf/day, >$15B CapEx
  • Power opportunity backlog: ~6 GW
  • Hyperscaler capex by 2030: ~$2T.

That makes the power platform more critical in the back half, and the update suggests that hyperscaler demand for Williams is very real. Buyers are not only trying out a concept; they are increasing existing commitments, extending terms and requesting more custom load-following solutions. I'd say that investors were looking for this kind of adjacent growth, rooted in gas transport and turbine operations, but different enough to earn a higher multiple if it’s executed properly. That being said, I usually caution against complacency regarding competition, and this is no different. The benefits of early market entry into behind-the-meter gas generation are real now, however, if these returns continue being that attractive, there will likely be additional entrants to that market. To defend against this level of competition, Williams has the following advantages: fast response times, availability of integrated gas supply resources, operational expertise, and a high level of trust with its customers. It ultimately comes down to whether these supposed advantages materialize when the first projects go live.

As I write this, markets are surging on ceasefire news. Oil is selling off, but I’m not ready to call this a resolution. The final whistle hasn’t blown. Let's assume, for now, that the reality around U.S. LNG exports is that global buyers will continue searching for replacement supply, courtesy of the Middle East mess, establishing that there is pull for exports as well as pull from power and pipeline markets. Now, while a new two-week ceasefire in the war with Iran has pushed oil prices down massively as traders have priced in a modestly temporary reopening of Hormuz, thus representing an appropriate backdrop for Williams.

The reduction in oil prices changes the circumstances a bit, but overall, it doesn't alter the validity of the thesis; Williams is selling contracted gas infrastructure and contracted power reliability. They're not chasing oil prices. If anything, I'm going to say, based on the back and forth between the Trump admin and Iran, that's it's fair to call this a fragile ceasefire, along with continued excessive physical market stress, reinforces that customers want secure delivery of domestic gas, storage, and power, even when oil price indices go down.

WMB: Growth vs. Valuation Dynamics

Based on my usual go-to conventional measures, WMB looks to be very overpriced. Essentially, the market's clearly paying much more than what's been realized in actual cash or earnings still to come. Fast Graphs shows a blended P/E of 34.26 versus a fair value P/E of 15.00 and normal P/E of 28.72.

This point alone tells us it's overvalued, especially given that the projected outlook has WMB producing a -11.37% expected annualized return and estimating fair value at approximately $32.41, while the current share price is now ~ $72.

Moving on to Seeking Alpha's metrics, we really just get the same story, only with more evidence. Non-GAAP earnings for WMB are currently valued at a very high level as the average sector multiple is 35.26x, compared to the sector 16.79x and WMB's 5-year average of 23.59x. On a forward basis, WMB's non-GAAP P/E is likewise high at 32.32x compared to the sector median of 14.38.

Finally, the metrics for WMB's EV/EBITDA are appreciably higher than the respective sector averages with the stock currently priced at 18.04x on a trailing period and 14.72x on a forward period, both of which are more than double sector averages.

The pushback is clearly growth.

Revenue growth's running at 10.02%, EBITDA growth at 21.10%, EBIT growth at 31.18%, and forward long-term EPS growth is pegged at 22.69%.

But we have some problems here.

Capex growth is 86.67%, free cash flow per share growth is projected at negative 23.98%, and long-term debt to capital is at 64.86%. This should clearly indicate to us that any premium will only work if the project pipeline converts to earnings as anticipated. At the multiples indicated above, even if execution is totally solid, if cash flows lag behind projections, and if there is increasing market demand for proof instead of promises, then the stock could be in trouble.

Final Verdict

All things considered, I'm going to Hold off for now on buying shares. I like the company. I also see a strong business with credible growth ahead. But the potential upside, considering the recent breakout run, seems a lot more limited now compared to the downside. I would consider buying WMB only if you specifically want a reliable, long-term gas infrastructure company and are okay paying a higher price for that stability and predictability.

data.title: Samsung: Q1 Profit Guide Well Ahead Of Consensus, Remains A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

I recently penned a piece on the memory maker Micron (MU), in that article I highlighted that fears relating to a new memory compression algorithm at Alphabet/Google (GOOGL, GOOG) known as TurboQuant, was unlikely to change the high level buy thesis on the memory market. Right on cue, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) provided market guidance of their revenue and profit ahead of their full earnings release later this month.

The numbers are pretty staggering. The guide showed the Korean chipmaker will deliver an 8x increase in operating profit year-on-year. A large increase in profit was of course expected, but the magnitude far exceed Wall Street estimates. The consensus analyst estimate was for 40.6T Won of operating profit, Samsung delivered 57.2T Won. This is a clear indication that memory is booming like we have never seen before. The market is experiencing a highly unusual supply deficit which is spearheaded by high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, a key requirement for AI.

The shift to HBM in AI workloads has gobbled up available wafer capacity in the industry, HBM can require up to 4x as many wafer as traditional DRAM. This has meant a dual aspect of incredible AI demand for memory, coupled with a structural change to the chip architecture that actually acts to reduce available supply.

The implication of this is an enormous increase in industry prices and profit at Samsung far in excess of market expectations. The memory market looks structurally tight with no near-term fix for the imbalance, this will only serve to continue to deliver out-sized profits for Samsung. I reiterate my buy rating on the stock following their Q1 profit guidance that eclipsed expectations.

Profits

I had a sense given what I have read about the memory market that Samsung would likely beat analyst estimates. After all, those of us who have followed markets for sometime appreciate the game that is analyst forecast numbers. Usually analyst estimates at at least somewhat informed by conversations with executives or investor relations people within companies they cover. Usually analysts are guided to a set of forecast number that companies fell confident they can beat. That's why when you look at the percentage of companies that “beat” on earning, it is usually around 70% or so.

This is an entirely deliberate and constructed exercise. It also explains why we often see stocks fall despite beating on their earnings estimates, the market says yeah we expected a beat but it wasn't enough, it was a “real” beat.

Needless to say, Samsung's beat this week was a “real” beat. Their operating profit number was almost a full 50% more than consensus estimates. This is a huge difference to expectations and it speaks to a situation in which analysts have simply not been able to keep pace with the speed and scale of earnings improvement as memory prices have skyrocketed.

The tightness of supply in the industry suggests to me that these elevated levels of profit are going to persist for several quarters to come. Industry participants have started engaging into longer-term contracts with Samsung, a step which is historically unusual for an industry accustomed to buying in the spot market or very short term supply contracts.

The implication of this longer-term contracts is greater visibility into earnings for Samsung and less likelihood of a sudden drop off. It also speaks to a sense of desperation at end customers, many of whom are leading hyperscalers competing fiercely with their rivals to develop the best AI systems. The intensity of competition positions Samsung well for the medium-term as a supplier of a much sought after and limited availability product.

Valuation

As I noted in my recent Micron piece, the valuation of memory companies can be a bit tricky on an earnings basis due to the huge cyclicality inherent in the business models. Looking below at a P/E chart of Samsung over the past five years the stock looks incredibly cheap. Surely its a easy buy called based on that valuation, right?

Unfortunately, earnings in the sector are simply too volatile to depend upon as a valuation signal. For instance, in the chart below the P/E spiked to 40x in early 2023. This spike was caused by a 96% decline in Samsung's quarterly profit as memory went through its last market trough. A P/E range of around 5x all the way up to 40x is not a useful indication of the true underlying value of a business.

The more appropriate choice for memory is the use of the price-to-book ratio. As I have noted before, the P/B ratio has its flaws, the biggest of which is its an inherently backward looking metric as it looks at the accounting equity value of a firm rather than a forward projection like expected earnings generation. However, despite its imperfections it is often employed in the case of memory. It notably tells us the opposite story of the P/E, the P/B tells us Samsung is on the expensive side at the moment.

If this were a normal market cycle I think this expensive valuation would give me a signal to pause and potentially move Samsung to a hold recommendation. However, the qualitative facts about the memory market at present and the sheer numbers Samsung is pumping out make a slightly elevated P/B ratio more than palatable.

The way I see it, it makes logical sense the stock should be on the expensive side, if it wasn't I think naturally one would have to ask what are we missing, if the firm is generating such huge profit in a highly supply constrained industry and the valuation isn't slightly elevated perhaps that would signal the market is clued into something we are missing.

Risks

The first risk to note is the aforementioned Alphabet's (GOOGL) Google TurboQuant. We always must be modest when facing into market, especially so when it pertains to rapidly changing sectors such as tech. I have a strong conviction that Jevon's Paradox will hold and their greater algorithmic efficiency in the use of memory will merely lead to even more memory being utilized in core AI workloads. Maybe I am wrong. First, maybe Jevon's Paradox, which held nicely for GPUs after the release of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) in early 2025, will not hold in the case of memory. I cannot see a reason why they would behave differently but that's the thing with investing, things can surprise you.

What I see as a more credible threat in relation to TurboQuant is it enhances the cyclicality of memory. Maybe TurboQuant has little impact on memory demand as we are in this AI led upswing, a moment of voracious need for any memory developers can get their hands on. But what happens on the flip side, as I noted in 2023 Samsung was a 96% decline in its profits in 2023 as the memory market went into a slump. If TurboQuant allows memory uses to do more with less then does that imply that the downswing of this cycle will be more severe that prior cycles, quite possibly.

The main thing to remember is that that risk is not a 2026 or even 2027 risk. The memory cycle looks well underpinned by robust demand and longer-term contracts, which should insulate the cycle for at least the medium-term.

Conclusion

We are in an unprecedented moment for the global memory market, and Samsung's profit numbers speak for themselves. Position sizing is key in a segment with the high cyclicality of memory, peaks and troughs are a feature not a bug in this market. All indications seem to point to a multiyear boom; the demand side looks set to continue to grow aggressively, and the supply side will be slow to catch up, partly become chip-makers will be wary of overbuilding.

The unusual use of longer-term pricing contracts for memory makes me think the market has legs to keep running. It signals the those seeking access to memory are willing to lock themselves in even if they face overpaying in the event of a decline in spot prices. This dynamic, to my mind, tells us the end users of memory are more concerned right now about supply availability than unit costs. This sets up a healthy situation of memory makers, and as a result I am reiterating my Buy rating on Samsung today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fiserv: New Management, New Markets, New Hope
data.description:
Co-written with Zach Gordon

Thesis

Fiserv (FISV) is undervalued. Slowing revenue growth and tumultuous management change have led to the stock falling nearly ~70% from its ATH in under a year. However, the market is underestimating the feasibility of the One Fiserv plan, along with promising opportunities in new markets that will drive strong growth into the future and support a stable operating model that prioritizes long-term profitability over short-term stock price. For these reasons, I assign a Buy rating with a ~90% potential upside, as I see such points coming to fruition.

Company Overview

Fiserv produces a variety of infrastructure with many uses across commerce/banking ecosystems, with examples including:

Merchant and Payment Platforms (Clover/Carat): These physical POS systems and payment platforms allow merchants to accept payment in store and online across different card networks.

Processing Infrastructure & Services (Core Banking): Provide backbone services to banks, things such as transaction routing, authorization, and settlement (how banks move money across networks).

Software and Data Solutions: Software converts transaction and customer data into real-time analytics, allowing banks/businesses to make smarter decisions regarding their finances.

The company operates on a contractual basis with most of its clients (2025 10-K Page 88), with much of its value coming from the hold it has on many of the top US banks and beyond, being able to cement itself as a leader in the industry through buyouts of other large companies and extensive relationships with many of its customers. The company caters to both larger institutions and small businesses, having both physical POS systems for restaurant/store usage and larger payment platforms for banks to manage, control, and route transactions along with internal Fiserv teams.

Merchant and financial solutions constitute the vast majority of revenue streams (2025 10-K Page 35), with the Clover platform leading the way as a high-margin, high-growth payment platform used by merchants across the US and beyond. The key thing to take away from this is that Fiserv still sees and will continue to see growth across its wider business model even with past variability of organic demand, which will be explained later. These are strong segments with ingrained and sustained demand owed to deep customer relationships and an extensive M&A history that ensures future expansion of the company.

The One Fiserv Plan: A new face for the company

In late 2025, Fiserv saw the largest management change in its history, with a new CEO (Mike Lyons), new CFO (Paul Todd), and new co-presidents (Takis G. and Dhivya S.), to name a few. New CEO Mike Lyons then told investors that current expectations would have to be revised, and this trend of lower revision and guidance regarding revenue growth has followed Fiserv all the way into the new year, with the company now expecting 1-3% 2026 growth. Through all of this, the stock experienced an extreme downturn, dropping nearly 80% from its ATH in only half a year.

Some of the prior YoY growth can be attributed to the runaway inflation that was seen in Argentina during 2023-2025. Large inflation rates in Argentina boosted growth rates, a phenomenon that management failed to clarify, leading to the eventual depression of guidance and revenue growth that we see now. Prior management had seemingly adopted a structure of short-termism within the company, prioritizing EPS and current quarter revenue growth over long-term profitability. In any case, a complete loss of trust and following change of leadership has caused the market to price in a sizable amount of risk following the changes. Investors see no reason to believe in a company where investor trust has been muddled following multiple revisions to growth expectations, leading to the enormous drop that we saw last year.

As you can see from the graph above, although revenue and net income remain largely stagnant, the sheer size of the drop in stock price may indicate that the market is acting on momentum, rather than fundamentals, a phenomenon likely driven by the largely untested capability of their replacements. However, management has assured investors that a focused and efficient future lies ahead, as technology hierarchy is modernized and the company reorients its model.

Along with this, heavy reinvestment and decreasing share repurchases support the words already spoken by executives in Q4 of last year, as Fiserv continues to look at modernizing and improving the efficiency of its current technology through collaborations with Microsoft Copilot and beyond. The willingness of management to act on its words through the One Fiserv plan will implant confidence and stability into a market that currently struggles to trust the struggling company, reorienting the market and the company towards a better path, and one that will drive profitability long into the future.

Barriers to entry and switching costs provide a large moat

As said previously, Fiserv acts on a contractual basis (usually long-term (5-10 years)) with major banking clients. As a result, they dominate the U.S. core banking market while also maintaining a strong presence in other verticals, those being card networking, payment processing, account management, and other financial operations. Fiserv is deeply embedded in each of these operations; switching to other providers means interrupting revenue and often takes months and often involves replacing 10-15 interconnected payment systems simultaneously.

Fiserv already finds itself ingrained in the banking system, being one of the largest FinTech providers in the U.S. with over 10,000 institutional clients (1 in 3 U.S. financial institutions) and 3,300+ credit unions (#1 in the U.S.). 95% of the top banks in the U.S. use Fiserv for differing applications, meaning Fiserv maintains a broad presence, holding a strong competitive position across key segments of the payments ecosystem and supporting recurring revenue and long-term client retention.

Similarly, Fiserv's large scale and substantial FCF allow them to invest more in both the company and acquisitions of competitors and promising companies. This creates a positive feedback loop in which Fiserv is consistently acquiring more companies and investing in more products than its competitors can, ensuring it retains the status of being one of the largest and most innovative companies in the FinTech and financial services industries.

Apart from this already wide moat, it is also apparent that in order for another FinTech company to emerge into the current market, they would need to comply with the vast amounts of regulations, including OCC, FDIC, FFIEC, and all following state-level banking regulations. Fiserv has the name-brand recognition that most startup companies do not, already making them the cemented giant of a market that is already difficult to break into. Following the First Data acquisition in 2019, Fiserv gained even more of an upper hand on competitors as it could then bundle both core banking software and merchant services into a single ecosystem, something most other competitors cannot accomplish.

The picture that I'm trying to portray here is that Fiserv already has the means necessary to expand its business, just as it already has for most of its lifetime. The market suddenly seeming to value it at much less than it previously has is nothing more than a momentary blip as we see new management settle into their roles. Nothing fundamental, moat-based or otherwise, has changed with regard to the fundamentals and effectiveness of the company's business model. Fiserv will continue to expand into different vectors (government, healthcare) as it continues to partner with large banks and companies, ensuring consistent revenue growth and expansion as it maintains its #1 position in the FinTech market and continues to show activity in M&A and internal improvements.

The Growing Stablecoin Market Presents A Unique Opportunity

Stablecoins, a type of cryptocurrency designed to maintain a stable value by pegging it to a reserve asset (USDC pegged to the US dollar), represent a "largely untapped" opportunity in the financial space, as they are still unregulated and have yet to be standardized and applied as largely as other common transactional methods, those being cash or card. However, stablecoin growth is very real, with stablecoin payment flows projected to reach $56.6 trillion by 2030 ($27.4 trillion in 2025). Stablecoins are rising in popularity largely due to ease of use with regard to cross-border transactions, and the ability to have a payment/deposit settled in seconds, not days.

Launched in December 2025 with the Stone Castle acquisition, FIUSD is Fiserv's dollar-pegged stablecoin, representing their stake in this growing market. Fiserv is uniquely positioned to scale FIUSD adoption because it already provides the core banking infrastructure used by thousands of banks across its networks, allowing FIUSD to be integrated directly instead of requiring banks to acquire new technology or switch to new platforms with other providers. Even fractional fee capture (transaction fees, usage fees) could represent enormous revenue upside as the volume of stablecoin usage only continues to grow.

The current cross-border payment settlement system is extremely inefficient, requiring buyers and sellers to meet many operational and compliance requirements regarding certain types of banks to be used and currency exchanges, causing opportunity cost risks and leaving much to be desired in terms of the speed and efficiency of the transaction. stablecoins eliminate many of these issues, as stablecoin transactions settle in seconds, have extremely cheap transaction costs, and have 24/7/365 availability. I believe Fiserv is well-positioned to be at the epicenter of this growth, with the release of its own stablecoin strengthening this sentiment.

Financials

Regarding margins, Fiserv retains the strongest GAAP and adjusted operating margins, reporting a 37.4% AOM (see Q4 ER) in 2025, representing a slight compression from ~39% in 2024. This is expected as we see larger reinvestment and a focus on restructuring the company after a period of weaker execution and compression under prior management. Despite this, the company maintains a high level of FCF ($4.44 billion in 2025) and a higher FCF yield (~14%) vs. peers, owed to its current level of efficiency and high margins as discussed previously. This will allow current reinvestment trends to continue as the company prioritizes stability and future growth over current profits.

Following this trend, I expect buybacks to decline in the following years after reaching a high in 2025 ($5.6 billion) as the company prioritizes deleveraging. Historically high leverage (~3x EBITDA range) is a concern, but the company is confident in its ability to weather this storm as it emphasizes deleveraging through use of its strong FCF and high-margin model.

Revenue was $21.19 billion in 2025, with organic revenue growth reaching 4%, and 2026 is being guided at 1-3%, a clear deceleration from prior years. Sustained revenue growth continues to be a pressing issue, especially after inflated figures drove up expectations from investors in previous quarters. However, merchant solutions (Clover) continues to show promising growth (~5-6%) and will likely drive revenue growth in future years as the company focuses on innovating and streamlining the platform by providing support/onboarding teams to clients and creating sales teams/modernizing systems to further support growth.

Strong cash conversion capabilities and a high-margin business model should help offset near-term execution issues as the Clover POS platform drives growth and creates a recurring, sticky revenue base that is already core to Fiserv's model. With core banking as a stable, slow growth segment, and Clover as a key growth driver, Fiserv will have the operational leverage it needs to accelerate growth and reverse current trends as we look to Q1 '26 and beyond.

Valuation

Given the current stock price of 53.90 as of 3/27/26 market close. Using a PGR of 2.25%, which is in line with current long-term GDP growth and appropriate for a mature and saturated company such as Fiserv, and a WACC of 9.75% (high equity risk driven by elevated beta counteracted by strong debt rating with low cost of debt), I reached an implied share price of $120.70, representing an upside of ~124%. However, given current execution and trust-related risks, I reached the decision to temper implied upside by 40% to 85-90% to better represent my thoughts on the current state of the company's operating model. Projections and margins are mostly averaged using historical years to guide projection year estimates, with revenue growth assumed to be flat in FY26 as the company restructures and prioritizes efficiency over growth.

I see revenue growth increasing by around Δ0.2-0.3% each year in the projection period until it reaches the perpetuity growth rate in the terminal year, with EBIT margins increasing by around 2.3% during the projection period and OpEx growing at 1% YoY, representing standard growth and reinvestment in the current model. Historical year revenue growth was obviously tempered for the projections to avoid extrapolating data from previous years (Argentina growth, Clover migration issues), hence the low sales CAGR from 2026-2030. Actual performance could exceed the estimates provided in the DCF if execution improves, but I wanted to provide a standard and somewhat conservative estimate for a company still reeling from the damages to investor/client trust that it saw last year.

Peer Analysis & Competition

The comp set maintains a relatively similar range in terms of market caps and other metrics, with the methodology in terms of choosing such competitors being the following:

  • Business Model/Revenue Drivers
  • Market Capitalization
  • Forward Multiples (EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales)
  • Margin Structure & Other Metrics

Key competitors include Jack Henry, PayPal, and Block, all giants with significant brand-name recognition in the financials space. I placed the most weight upon similarity of business models, as I believe that direct competitors are most important in terms of comparison, especially when Fiserv operates within many different segments. You will notice that Fiserv has significantly higher margins than the rest of the pack (~12% higher expected for 2026 and 2027), owed to its high-margin business model and wide moat that provides a large amount of pricing power, along with an extremely low P/E ratio owed to the trough that the current stock price finds itself in.

Fiserv trades at a massive discount compared to its peers (6.2x EV/EBITDA compared to 9.0x average for comps), an issue that doesn't have much at all to do with EBITDA margins or earnings, as previously stated. I believe Fiserv is trading at a notable discount, one that is not justifiable by the headwinds that it currently faces. Low sales growth and CAGR are the largest issues currently facing the company, and ones that will surely be accounted for and fixed as new management reorganizes the company, suggesting the current discount may in fact be excessive.

Risks

Merchant segment competition is also stiff, and with the Clover platform being one of Fiserv's largest growth drivers, this could spell trouble for the company in terms of consistent, long-term revenue growth. The SMB merchant space is hyper-competitive, meaning Fiserv will have to constantly innovate and improve its current platforms in order to remain relevant, a sentiment that is even more prevalent given its position as a leading competitor in the market. If pricing pressure and higher customer acquisition costs squeeze Clover out of the space, we could see the entire growth narrative breaking apart.

Lastly, the untested capability of management is certainly one of the largest risks facing the company at this point. Although most of the new management has ample experience in the finance industry, executives remain largely untested regarding their ability to run a company that has seen a shattered stock price and slowed revenue growth, with most being rushed into their positions and forced to make quick decisions that will have large impacts on the future of the company. We have yet to see how they will perform in such a chaotic environment, with time being the only tell as to if they will succeed.

Regional volatility and the inability to predict and prevent situations such as those that were seen in the Argentina segment lie chief among Fiserv's risk. Fiserv reports revenue and transaction volume in nominal terms, meaning growth can be skewed if inflationary events such as the one described before happen again. The company's ability to correctly disclose one-off events lies in the competency of management, meaning confidence in new management is key with regard to sustained and steady revenue growth as we head into Q1 2026 and beyond.

Final Thoughts

There is no doubt Fiserv is in a challenging period. With executives racing to reorganize a company that was previously defined by unsustainable revenue growth and inefficient spending, it is certain there will be some growing pains with regard to new management and new expansions. However, I am confident that the current sentiment of the market is far overblown, as Fiserv remains a fundamentally sound company, and one that has plenty of cushion to drive growth far into the future. With a management committed to the revitalization of the company, and the multitude of opportunities for growth that Fiserv has in its future, I believe there is an effective path toward a secure future, with Fiserv emerging as a new company with a more disciplined operating outlook.

data.title: VinFast: Recent Rally Fuels Some Skepticism
data.description:
One of the market's biggest winners in recent weeks has been VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS). The Vietnamese electric vehicle and scooter maker has seen its shares surge more than 30% after reporting its fourth-quarter results. While the company is showing large revenue growth that's expected to continue this year, it is also racking up significant losses and cash burn that make me question the recent rally in shares.

Previous Coverage Of VinFast Auto

The last time I looked at VinFast was back in November 2025, right after the company had reported its third-quarter results. I liked the sales trajectory, but not the losses and cash burn. The balance sheet wasn't in great shape, but the company was receiving a lot of financial support from related parties. Since that time, shares have rallied more than 21%, compared to a small decline in the S&P 500, but VinFast remains well below where I first went to a sell rating and even further off its all-time highs.

VinFast Q4 Results And Recent Updates

In the final quarter of 2025, VinFast delivered over 86,550 electric vehicles, up 127% over the prior year period. Electric scooter and bike sales came in at nearly 172,000, up from just 31,000 in the prior year period. This led total revenues to come in at nearly $1.57 billion for Q4. Not only was this number up roughly 139% over Q4 2024, but it handily beat street estimates by around $439 million. In both dollar and percentage terms, this was the largest beat of analyst expectations in the company's public trading history.

The problem for VinFast is that it hasn't gotten its cost base under control just yet. The gross loss was over $626 million in Q4, an increase of more than 20% from the prior year period. The company's gross margin percentage did improve substantially from about negative 79% to negative 40%, but that was mainly a function of the revenue surge. The company reported an operating loss of more than $1.15 billion for Q4 2025, a nearly 22% year-over-year increase. The quarterly net loss came in at more than $1.4 billion, a 15% rise from Q4 2024.

When it comes to this year, VinFast is looking for more dramatic growth. The company is targeting 300,000 electric vehicle deliveries, up from less than 197,000 last year. The company has a maximum annual production capacity of 600,000 electric vehicles across multiple factories, which it certainly hopes to use more of in the coming years. E-scooter and bike sales are expected to be 2.5 times last year's total.

When it comes to expectations, street analysts are looking for more than 61% revenue growth this year to $5.80 billion. Double-digit billions are forecast for 2029 at the moment, but 2028 is in play if this growth story continues to beat estimates. Perhaps the biggest goal this year is to improve costs, both on the production and operating sides, as it looks to get those losses down. In the short term, the company can use the surge in global oil prices from the US/Iran War as a way to push electric choices to consumers.

Balance Sheet And Cash Flow Picture

With these large losses comes major cash burn for VinFast. For the full year, the company reported negative operating cash flow of more than $1.77 billion, and this was before another $921 million was spent on capex. Both of these numbers were up substantially from 2024 levels, which came in at $1.25 billion and $686 million, respectively. Hopefully as the business continues to scale in the coming years, cash burn will start to decrease.

VinFast finished last year with about $293 million in cash, and that number was down more than $167 million sequentially. Total debt also ticked up a couple of percent to more than $3.27 billion. Working capital remains deep in the red at more than $3.19 billion, but that number has actually improved by about $1.25 billion in the past three quarters. The company continues to receive major support through grants and loans from its founder and parent group, along with some select asset sales. VinFast finished last year with over $3 billion in liquidity, mostly from remaining grants, an undrawn credit line, and an outstanding equity subscription agreement.

VFS Stock - A Look At Current Valuations

With shares rallying a bit recently, the company's valuation has gotten a little stretched in my opinion. For this argument, let's discount Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) for the moment, as the EV leader goes for almost 13 times its expected revenues for this year. In the chart below, I've compared VinFast against several more comparable pure-play EV names - Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), NIO Inc. (NIO), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), and Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY)—based on current street revenue estimates for 2026. I excluded Tesla from the chart as it really skews the numbers with its sky-high valuation.

As of midday Wednesday trading, VinFast shares were trading at 1.59 times their street-expected revenue average for 2026. That puts them at the second highest of the seven non-Tesla names. If we look at the other six non-Tesla names, the average is just 1.27 times, so VinFast does trade at a bit of a premium. The growth story is better than some of these others, but the losses and cash burn are also higher than you'd like.

Wall Street analysts continue to be very positive when it comes to VinFast. Of the four that have covered the stock in the past three months, three have strong buy ratings, with the other being a hold rating. The average price target currently sits at $6.30, implying about 60% upside from current levels. That average has risen nearly 50 cents since my previous article, but it was in the double digits until mid-April of 2024.

Final Thoughts And Recommendation

VinFast shares have jumped recently after the company's large fourth-quarter revenue beat. While the company is showing tremendous sales growth currently, it is also seeing major and increasing losses and cash burn. The balance sheet remains in poor shape, but the company is getting major capital help from its founder and parent company for now.

With an above-average valuation against the non-Tesla electric vehicle names, I'm going to keep my sell rating on the stock here today. I do like the growth potential here, but the company needs to get its expenses in check sooner rather than later. If VinFast can start to make progress on some of these fronts in 2026, I can take another look at my rating then.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings Corporation: Good Turnaround Efforts But Not Sold On Momentum
data.description:
Hooker Furnishings Corporation (HOFT) has meaningfully dealt with the tariff issues that we foresaw in our previous coverage. Discontinuing certain businesses, they are more nearshored and have left some of their value categories where they now have more pricing power. Backlog is growing, margins are improving, restructuring will eventually move in the rearview, and new organic growth initiatives should be supporting the business with the launch of Margaritaville. Other tough comps for the hospitality business, which we understand to be a residual business after the intended disposal of Samuel Lawrence, are also moving into the rearview. The activists seem to be satisfied with all this. However, we do expect warehousing costs to now increase related to higher freight costs, and also the macro backdrop for key business drivers like real estate prices and homebuilding is going to be worse in coming quarters due to the reinflation theme in markets. Growing backlogs are good to see, but we aren't sold on the sales momentum being that great going forward and in the coming couple of quarters.

Last Results

Some of the key themes of the latest earnings were that there was a tough comp last year in the hospitality business, which is a big part of the "all other" segment as a consequence of the classification of Samuel Lawrence ex-hospitality as discontinued due to a letter of intent from a buyer for that business, and some other lines associated with lower value categories hit hard by the lost competitiveness from tariffs on China and Vietnam. The discontinued businesses were around 15% of consolidated sales, and now 40% of net sales are associated with domestically assembled/produced products. It's also a meaningful shift, also to higher-value categories that can take some competitiveness hits from the tariffs.

Other than that, the segments were seeing growth.

Volumes took a hit, but sales prices were up, so profit improvement was impressive in Hooker Branded despite almost flat sales. The backlog grew by 17.2% from the previous fiscal year and 8% sequentially.

The domestic upholstery business saw less constructive backlog development but was up YoY by 3.5%. Sales were up slightly, and gross profits also improved.

There are still significant restructuring costs on the businesses that is being phased out that could seriously improve incremental losses or thin incremental income. We'd start looking at this phaseout starting from the coming quarter. Long-term 10% operating margins apparently are an achievable target as per the company's historical margin, and with higher backlog this quarter for the higher-margin Hooker Branded segment, we are hoping for backlog liquidation to lift scale and margins through mix effects in the coming quarterly results. Additionally, the launch of the Margaritaville line in partnership with the global lifestyle brand, products of which the company expects will have a margin profile about the midpoint between Hooker Branded and the upholstery business, is also a new launch of a line that they hope will offset some of the lost scale and sales that occurred when disposing of their lower-value businesses. However, management expects contributions from this and the associated marketing to bear fruit in the second half of the coming year. In the coming quarter, the sale of the discontinued businesses will also have closed, adding around $6 million to the liquidity, which actually covers 20% of the overall net financial obligations.

So overall, we expect some volume improvement in the coming quarter thanks to the backlog build, higher margins thanks to that and the growth of Hooker-branded products in the mix, which lagged this last quarter on volume, easier comps, less restructuring costs, and a liquidity injection. The effects of the Iran War won't show until the quarter after that, as the last quarter closed on November 2.

Risks

In terms of the financial position, they have around $30 million in liabilities to worry about between leases and creditors, but around $63 million in a credit facility and a $6 million injection from sales of discontinued businesses. They have some degree of support, which we expect can continue as their profitability finally turns a corner. But even adding back restructuring, margins are very thin and the obligations quite considerable. A return to scale would be helpful for the business but is fraught by the new Iran War crisis, which is reintroducing inflation into the US economy, likely to lead to some demand destruction pressure, and also once again limit the housing market and the appetite for new space to fill with furniture. This will matter in the disclosures after next. Discretionary commercial markets are also going to be under pressure in the current reinflationary economic regime, in addition to being a backdrop with growth threats due to low hire and fire and AI threats to the white-collar workforce. While the announcement of a conditional ceasefire depending on the Strait reopening is possibly a great immediate relief, details are still unclear, and logistic costs will likely still be elevated by Iran's control of the Strait, perhaps through their tolls.

Bottom Line

Things are improving, but considerable margin improvement after adding back impairments and restructuring costs will be needed. Volumes will need to turn around, which we see as possible, particularly with backlog trends. However, reinflation, already baked into the yield curve and into economic dynamics, is a concern. In terms of valuation, it's hard to say without run-rate profitability, but supposing a 10% EBIT on annualized sales, the EV/EBIT is around 6x, which is considerably more discounted than peers at more than double that (15.5x market cap weighted, or relative to peers in bedding at around 16x, Williams-Sonoma (WSM) at 14x forward EV/EBIT). However, we still wouldn't bet on that level of profitability until the clouds clear. Moreover, when considering real estate, we'd also strictly prefer in this case deep-value Canadian options such as Leon's Furniture (LNF:CA) and, to a lesser extent due to liquidity, the BMTC Group (GBT:CA), which have highly compressed EVs due to very significant real estate balances that are contemplated to be spun off. These companies don't have current profitability problems either and much lower current EV/EBIT multiples around 2-3x without needing to forecast a total profitability recovery like with HOFT.

data.title: Palantir: DevCon5 Shows A Disruptor, Not The Disrupted
data.description:
PLTR Stock and the "SaaSpocalypse"

I last analyzed Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) on Feb 25. That article served as a review for Palantir’s FQ4 earnings report (ER) and rated it as a Buy. Since then, several catalysts have evolved both around the macroeconomy and also PLTR’s specific operations. In the subsequent sections of this article, I will focus on the top change as I see it in each bucket. For the former, I will focus on the selloff in the broader market, especially the overall software sector, caused by the SaaSpocalypse fear and also the geopolitical uncertainties. For the latter, I will focus on the business and product updates presented in its recent PLTR’s fifth Developer Convention (DevCon5).

Let’s start with the macroscopic changes. Since my last writing, the broader market (e.g., SPY shown in the next chart) has suffered a noticeable correction due to the breakout of the Iran conflicts and changes in commodity-linked assets. These issues, when combined with the SaaSpocalypse fear (i.e., the fear of agentic AI disrupting software businesses), have caused a far more severe pullback for the software industry. For example, the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) has experienced a sharp correction of more than 24% in the past 3 months. PLTR, as IGV’s top holding, also suffered a 17% retreat.

Next, I will argue why the indiscriminate selloff in the software sector has confused PLTR’s role in the incoming agentic AI era. I will explain why I see it as a disruptive force, not a business to be disrupted by the agent AI revolution.

PLTR Stock: Fifth Developer Convention (DevCon5)

This brings me to the updates provided in PLTR’s fifth Developer Convention (DevCon5). As shown in the next chart below, DevCon5 focused on the growing power of AI agents and how PLTR helps to deploy them at scale across complex organizations ranging from commercial enterprises to government agencies. Based on the new capabilities demonstrated at DevCon5, I consider PLTR as a key disruptor in the agentic AI age, rather than one to be disrupted by the multitude of AI tools offered by other developers. Many of the capacities showcased in the conference are beyond generic LLM (large language models) in my view, especially in complex, highly regulated, and mission-critical environments.

The best example is the Orchestrator tool. Based on the capabilities showcased, the first impression that came to my mind is that with Orchestrator, Palantir isn't just selling AI tools. With Orchestrator, PLTR is selling enterprise autonomy. In this autonomy, PLTR’s tools provide agents that are long-running (for example, tasks spanning across hours or days), interruptible by allowing developers to pause parts of workflows indefinitely without cascading failures, and observable by providing live feedback to developers. In a nutshell, these features are the most fundamental and also crucial hallmarks of a human employee – a capable one, I might add – demonstrates for complicated tasks such as medical treatment or military operation.

PLTR Stock: Valuation Check

Thanks to the above differentiating capabilities, the market sees rapid EPS growth for PLTR ahead. As an example, the current consensus EPS estimates point to a CAGR of around 36% in the next 5 years. Admittedly, despite the rapid growth rates and also the recent share price pullback, the stock is still trading at a triple-digit P/E ratio of 113x, as seen in the snapshot below.

However, I recommend potential investors, especially those with an extended time horizon (say 3~5 years), to look beyond the accounting P/E ratios for several reasons. First, as argued in my earlier articles, for a rapidly growing company like PLTR, the accounting P/E ratios tend to exaggerate their valuation risks as the company spends aggressively on growth CAPEX. As a reflection of how (and how much) the accounting EPS can be misleading, the chart below shows the ratio between PLTR’s FCF (free cash flow per share) and its accounting EPS in the past 5 years. As seen, since mid-2023, the FCF/EPS ratio has been consistently and significantly above its accounting EPS. The ratio peaked at almost 4x in late 2023, hovered around 2x for most of 2024 and 2025, and now sits at about 1.3x.

Second, the current P/E ratio, as off-putting as it seems, has good odds of generating a positive return even with a substantial valuation contraction factored in. As a simple back-of-the-envelope estimate, the chart below shows my simple reality check on the key variables involved in a growth stock: the entry P/E, growth rates, and also exit P/E. In such an estimate, I am particularly interested in the duration that I need to hold the shares to realize a given annual return (say 10%), as time compounds uncertainty.

In PLTR’s case, with an entry P/E of 113x and expected CAGR growth rates in the range of 35% to 40%, potential investors would need to hold the shares for about 4 years to realize a 10% annual ROI return on the stock when the terminal P/E is 28x, which is the current ratio for the SP500 index. Even with an exit P/E of 22x, which is the current ratio for the Information Technology sector, the holding period is about 5 years. These are my typical investment timeframes and are within my acceptable zone (although they are approaching the limit of my comfort zone for a sector that is experiencing rapid evolution).

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Besides the valuation risks, a few other downside risks are worth noting. The level of technical superiority shown in DevCon5 can be a double-edged sword. In the tech world, superiority often comes with a side effect of complexity, which can potentially cause operational friction and scaling difficulty. As a reflection, the reviews gathered below by Gartner suggest a steep initial learning curve for PLTR’s products, significant time investment for customization, etc. These issues could make Palantir look more like a specialized consultant than a scalable SaaS company. If every new client requires a team of engineers to "map the Ontology" and configure the Orchestrator, Palantir may struggle to scale as rapidly as the consensus EPS projections imply.

To close, this PLTR revisit is largely motivated by two new changes since my last writing: the broader market correction and also the product updates provided in its DevCon5. These developments have led me to view the recent selloff of PLTR shares together with the overall software sector as an entry opportunity. I believe the selloff reflects a misjudgment on PLTR’s role in the incoming agentic AI era. I see PLTR as a disruptor, not the disrupted one in the ongoing AI revolution.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Don't Let This Buying Opportunity Go To Waste
data.description:
Rocket Lab: Neutron Delay Hasn't Yet Cratered the Bullish Narrative

To be honest, I actually seldom pay close attention to the rocket companies. While I know they may represent the futuristic side of growth investing, I'm not really that speculative an investor.

Because these companies are arguably solving some of the hardest problems that humanity faces, the execution risks are also extremely high.

So just like I do not like to buy very short-duration call options (as compared to selling a cash-secured put), I don't particularly envy investors who may have made a ton of money investing in Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB).

That said, RKLB investors will probably contend that it's a case of sour grapes, right?

Consider that RKLB still maintained a total return of almost 280% over the past year.

You must consider that the multiples are seemingly extreme, at 44x on the forward P/S ratio, compared to the sector median of 1.8x.

Surprisingly, I have not seen a structurally bearish print on RKLB yet, giving due credence to the high level of confidence in the progress that Rocket Lab has attained so far.

Yet, that doesn’t mean that RKLB could avoid falling into a bear market, with the stock having lost almost 45% through the recent lows after peaking in January this year.

If you have been following Rocket Lab’s developments closely, I guess you will probably agree with me that the disappointment with the delayed Neutron launch (pushed back to Q4 this year) played a prominent role in the recent derating.
