Like I mentioned earlier, these are the kinds of risks the investors in RKLB or any other so-called disruptive rocket company trying to gain a foothold against the legacy defense prime must assume.

It's rocket science, right? Therefore, even though Rocket Lab posted what seemed like a solid Q4 performance, the market was, well, nonchalant.

Rocket Lab's Execution Risks on Neutron Are Immensely High, Nonetheless

The focus will always be on Neutron, despite having already proven its launch capabilities with Electron.

This makes sense because for the company to move forward and handle increasingly large payloads, then success with Neutron is not optional. It should read as critical, in my opinion.

That’s already very obvious if you care to pay attention to what the company indicated in its annual filings, which I think is self-explanatory.

So I guess we can understand why the market was disappointed, despite clear progress made in the rest of the business, and even posted a new record for Electron launches.

The fact is that RKLB doesn’t have a proven business model just yet.

Sure, I agree that costs per launch have been markedly lowered, while revenue per launch has also increased. The economics of improved scale helping RKLB find a sustainable path toward long-term viability has been duly lifted.

But, it’s not as if the market hasn’t rated it for growth, right? We already know that the valuation is by no means cheap.

Management has also attempted to assure investors that they wouldn’t keep on disappointing. However, it’s Q4 (for Neutron launch) that we are talking about, yeah? So until then, some skepticism cannot be avoided, no matter what.

And since these investors have a SpaceX (SPACE) IPO that looks to be coming soon to the public markets, could it also shift the narrative in favor of the Elon Musk-led firm?

Nevertheless, Musk appears to also have faced some untimely snag on Starship, with another test expected to happen sometime in May. That’s not too far away, mind you, and definitely coming well before RKLB’s debut.

Rocket Lab: Strong Backlog and Potential Earnings Growth Inflection Are Crucial

Yet, I think we must also give credit when it's due, as RKLB has made decent progress, with backlog rising to $1.85B, a 69% QoQ increase.

But this is also mostly linked to the “SDA Tranche III Tracking Layer” award, so investors shouldn’t assume that such increases will be a regular occurrence.

It’s more important to pay attention to monetization, as I think with SpaceX going into the public sphere (while anticipated to raise almost $80B in the process), competition on future launches could intensify.

In some ways, it should put pressure on RKLB to deliver on its Neutron launch immaculately in Q4. Any further disappointment wouldn’t go down well with investors, whatever the reason may be, I think.

Wall Street is also keeping a close tab on RKLB’s ability to turn profitable.

Based on the revised estimates, Rocket Lab could deliver positive adjusted EPS for FY2027. That will be a monumental achievement.

And we know that fixed cost leverage in this business is the primary growth driver and earnings pillar.

With reusability, that becomes an immense force multiplier and something that even NASA administrator Jared Isaacman has also emphasized recently.

Therefore, while the company has made solid progress so far, it needs to validate Neutron. At the current valuations, there’s no room for error.

But since Q4 is still 2 quarters away, can the stock withstand further selling pressure while buyers find a consolidation zone to buy in?

For now, I think investors are also growing more confident that it has been able to raise equity at a high valuation, bolstering the much-needed liquidity.

I think such moves provide more time for RKLB to keep investors onside while they work out the kinks and let Neutron potentially outshine peers over time.

I think it's fair to say that the company has proven its launch capabilities while also inking more valuable deals, as seen with the growing backlog.

But the long-term prize will very much depend on how successful Neutron could launch and ramp, and that’s something that investors must assume today, although the outlook is still highly uncertain.

Buy or Sell RKLB Stock?

To be clear, the RKLB print is still holding up very well, just in case you thought the stock was about to crash further.

I'm also surprised as well. I guess it goes to show that the market is confident enough about its potential to nail the launch by Q4, as highlighted.

Of course, it now depends on its execution, but buyers seem to have stayed firmly in place to accumulate.

I assessed a support zone above the $55 level. Incidentally, that level has also been holding steadfastly for at least 2 months, which is good.

Because for the bullish bias to stay intact, the 50-week moving average must be supported (blue line).

Although there is a deeper consolidation zone somewhere under the $40 level, I don't see it being a focal point to induce further selling right now.

Therefore, to a large extent, it does seem to me that the market is leaning confidently on RKLB resuming its advance from here.

For investors who set aside a speculative pot to invest in space stocks, this might be an opportunity to add exposure if you haven’t.

Rating: Speculative Buy

data.title: Eni Returned Over 100% Since My Buy Call: Here Is How Much Upside Is Left
data.description:
Introduction to Eni

Chances are you traveled during Easter, and if you did so by car, you've felt the pain every time you had to fill up your car at the gas pump. Part of the relief might have come from your gains in case you were invested in oil stocks. But the news of the ceasefire in Iran is even more welcome, as we hope the war comes soon to an end.

On this news, oil stocks are down several percentage points, and I want to use this drop to revisit one of my best calls in this space: E Eni S.p.A. (E), which I considered a buy in October 2024 and has since then returned over 100% (S&P 500 (SP500): +16%). At that time, I thought that Eni was particularly interesting, as it was developing from a pure upstream player into a company with key satellite businesses, such as Enilive (biofuels) and Plenitude (renewables), both of which were attracting funds at valuations that basically implied that Eni's stock was trading at a big discount if we used a sum-of-the-parts valuation model (basically, its upstream business was priced at 3 EV/EBIT).

The repricing happened and, pay attention, it was not entirely triggered by the recent war in Iran. It started much earlier as confirmation signals from the satellite business made investors realize that Eni was trading at a discount, as we see below. Indeed, Eni more than doubled its price returns (not counting the generous yield, which was almost 8% less than two years ago and which still is close to 6% if we consider its buybacks).

Eni might not be familiar to some of my readers, especially those who don't live in Europe. It is one of the largest European oil companies, and it is headquartered in Rome (the Italian government has a 30.5% stake). Its upstream business represents the core of the company, generating around 90% of the company's adj. EBIT. Eni's portfolio is exposed to key African areas (Congo, Angola, Libya, Egypt, Cote d'Ivoire, Mozambique), as well as Kazakhstan and Indonesia.

Eni has a high replacement ratio of 167% (for every barrel produced, Eni replaces it with 1.67 barrels in proved reserves). This is a key metric for an oil company because it tells us that its resources are not depleting. Eni is also a key player in gas and LNG, being one of the largest European suppliers. Eni reports a global gas and LNG portfolio, but this doesn't include exploration and production; it is the trading and offtake business. Eni played a key role in replacing Russian gas with other suppliers.

The Satellite-Model

But Eni is developing a satellite business model, which is becoming cash-generative, with EUR 16B expected to come in from 2026 through 2030. The two main legs of this unit are Enilive and Plenitude, the former being a biofuel producer business, and the latter being a renewables and retail energy business. There is also the refining and chemicals business, which currently operates at a loss. This is why Eni is gradually repurposing its refineries from traditional operations to biorefineries.

Let's take a closer look at this part of the company.

In 2025, Eni Ares Management Alternative Credit funds, affiliates of Ares Management Corporation (ARES), signed an agreement for a 20% stake in Plenitude valued at around EURB (implying an equity valuation of EUR 10B, and an EV above EUR 12B).

Considering that the fund KKR acquired a 30% stake in Enilive at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 12, at the end of FY25 Eni reported over EUR 23B of EV highlighted by these satellite transactions.

Plenitude is not done. It has signed an agreement with Acea Energia, which should add 1.4M retail customers to its portfolio, so that Plenitude would reach its 11M goal originally set for 2028 two years ahead of schedule. There is a risk of regulator approval, but in a few months, we should know the final verdict.

Finally, the satellite model is growing because a carbon capture and storage division is growing. It is still in an early stage, but it has already attracted the capital of Global Infrastructure Partners (part of BlackRock), which holds a 49.99% stake in this business unit.

The pattern is clear. Eni identifies businesses with a high transition value, so that it can sign agreements with institutional investors that help validate the valuation. Eni holds control of the unit and generates cash flows that help it strengthen its balance sheet and fund its upstream growth.

Eni recently hit the news after it signed a €500M loan with the European Investment Bank to convert part of its Sannazzaro de' Burgondi's refinery (a facility doing 180,000 bbl/d) into a biorefinery, which further incentivizes me to update my sum-of-the-parts valuation and my general overview of this oil company that plays a key role in the Mediterranean and Africa. The production target for the biorefinery is 550,000 t/year of HVO diesel and SAF, starting in 2028. This is the second EIB loan in a year, after the one for the conversion of the Livorno refinery. Eni surely deserves institutional confidence, and this is why we have to understand that receiving an EIB loan has key advantages. In fact, these loans have rates that reflect EU sovereign credit instead of Eni's cost of capital. As a result, there is a clear advantage compared to corporate bond funding: rates are lower, and this allows the return rates to be higher. Moreover, this loan ties the debt to the conversion project, which means that the balance sheet of the company is protected.

Eni's Financials

If we look at Eni's FY25 results, it seems as if we are looking at a company reporting from ages ago: Brent averaged $69/bbl (FY24: $80/bbl). However, the stock held well and even moved up as the company showed operational resilience. Even though the adj. EBIT declined by 15% to EUR 12.22B; the two largest satellites, Enilive and Plenitude, reported a combined 6% EBIT growth to EUR 1.21B, representing now 10% of the company's overall EBIT. The adj. CFFO was down 8%, but was still strong: EUR 12.5B; capex remained under control, declining by 3 pps to EUR 8.5B. In the meantime, however, production increased by 7%, which was a strong signal: the business is growing while its costs are well managed. Installed renewable capacity increased by 41% YoY to 5.8GW.

All of this contributed to continued balance sheet improvements, which are shown below. The company always keeps around EUR 15B at hand, while its total debt decreased by EUR 2.5B YoY to EUR 34.2B, which means that its net debt/EBITDA ratio is around 1.5x. Keep in mind that, while deleveraging the balance sheet, Eni raised its dividends and increased its buybacks. Secondly, Eni lowered its gearing (the net debt/total capital ratio) to 14%, which means that only 14 cents of every euro Eni employs is financed, while the remaining 85 cents is equity.

For oil majors, gearing is extremely important because earnings are volatile (we've just seen a clear example of this, as oil prices soared and are now moving back down sharply). If gearing is too high, when the cycle moves south, the company could be in a dangerous position. Low gearing, on the other hand, allows a company to have the flexibility to invest throughout every phase of the cycle, while staying committed to the dividend and being able to face any negative shocks without too much fear.

In an environment where oil prices (CO1:COM) are volatile and are still close to $100 per barrel, it can be easy to think that Eni's distributions will likely increase this year, with an extra dividend and extra buyback that could be announced down the road on top of the EUR 1.5B buyback announced in mid-March. For sure, compared to the past, the company has now raised its ambitions and wants to return between 35% and 45% of its operating cash compared to the previous target (35%-40%), with an extra dividend being paid if Brent prices, on average, stay above $90.

Eni's Valuation

Is Eni a deal right now? When I say this, I am not going to argue whether the stock will drop or will rise in the next few months. Most of this will depend on what happens between the U.S. and Iran. But I want to see whether the case I built a year and a half ago still holds. Eni retains 70% of Enilive and 65% of Plenitude, which means that the combined enterprise value given the deals with KKR and ARES is around EUR 16.2B. We can then consider its gas and power business unit, whose EBIT is around EUR 1.4B. Using a multiple of 8, we have an estimated value of EUR 11.1B. Refining and chemicals are basically zero, while the nascent carbon business, where GIP has a stake, could be valued around EUR 500M.

We can now consider Eni's core business: exploration and production. Considering that it brings in around 11.2B in adj EBIT and its EBITDA could be around EUR 15.7B (I estimate 4.5B in D&A), with a 4.5 multiple, we have an EV of EUR 70.4B. The total is close to EUR 100B. We can then subtract the net debt of EUR 19B, and we have an equity value of EUR 81B. The shares outstanding are 2.9B, so the fair price is EUR 27.9. At the time of writing, the stock trades at EUR 23.50 in Milan, which means that the upside has thinned, but a nice 19% is still there. The ADR is current $55.10, but the target price in EUR equals to $63.1 (at EUR/USD 1.13; each ADR represents 2 shares), which reduces the upside to 14.5%.

There is, however, one catalyst that could materialize: Plenitude's IPO. Deconsolidation has already been announced, and if an IPO follows, we could see renewed interest among investors to have a stake in Eni.

I think we are close to fair value territory because the margin of safety here is thinner. The valuation could change if Brent prices stay above $100. But in the base scenario where the war ends and oil prices gradually normalize, I think Eni is now close to its intrinsic value and, therefore, I rate it as a Hold.
